title: शनि दशरथ कृत स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शनि दशरथ कृत स्तोत्रम् ॥

ॐ अस्य श्रीशनैश्चरस्तोत्रस्य दशरथ ऋषिः।
शनैश्चरो देवता। त्रिष्टुप् छन्दः।
शनैश्चरप्रीत्यर्थे जपे विनियोगः॥

कोणस्थः पिंगलो बभ्रुः कृष्णो रौद्रोऽन्तको यमः।
सौरिः शनैश्चरो मन्दः पिप्पलादेन संस्तुतः॥ १॥

एतानि दश नामानि प्रातरुत्थाय यः पठेत्।
शनैश्चरकृता पीडा न कदाचिद्भविष्यति॥ २॥

दशरथ उवाच:
नमः कृष्णाय नीलाय शितिकण्ठनिभाय च।
नमः कालाग्निरूपाय कृतान्ताय च वै नमः॥ ३॥

नमो निर्मांसदेहाय दीर्घश्मश्रुजटाय च।
नमो विशालनेत्राय शुष्कोदरभयाकृते॥ ४॥

नमः पुष्कलगात्राय स्थूलरोम्णेऽथ वै नमः।
नमो दीर्घाय शुष्काय कालदंष्ट्र नमोऽस्तु ते॥ ५॥

नमस्ते कोटराक्षाय दुर्निरीक्ष्याय वै नमः।
नमो घोराय रौद्राय भीषणाय करालिने॥ ६॥

नमस्ते सर्वभक्षाय वलीमुख नमोऽस्तु ते।
सूर्यपुत्र नमस्तेऽस्तु भास्करे भयदाय च॥ ७॥

अधोदृष्टे नमस्तेऽस्तु संवर्तक नमोऽस्तु ते।
नमो मन्दगते तुभ्यं निस्त्रिंशाय नमोऽस्तु ते॥ ८॥

प्रसादं कुरु मे सौरे वरदोऽसि ममेश्वर।
एतैर्नामभिरुक्तैश्च स्तुतवान् यश्च मानवः।
तस्य नैव भवेत्पीडा प्रीतो भव दयानिधे॥ ९॥

॥ इति श्री शनि दशरथ कृत स्तोत्रम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Shani Dasharatha Krita Stotram ||

Om Asya Shri Shanaishchara Stotrasya Dasharatha Rishih |
Shanaishcharo Devata | Trishtup Chhandah |
Shanaishchara Prityarthe Jape Viniyogah ||

Konasthah Pingalo Babhruh Krishno Raudro'ntako Yamah |
Saurih Shanaishcharo Mandah Pippaladena Samstutah || 1 ||

Etani Dasha Namani Pratar Utthaya Yah Pathet |
Shanaishchara Krita Pida Na Kadachid Bhavishyati || 2 ||

Dasharatha Uvacha:
Namah Krishnaya Nilaya Shitikantha Nibhaya Cha |
Namah Kalagnirupaya Kritantaya Cha Vai Namah || 3 ||

Namo Nirmamsa Dehaya Dirgha Shmashru Jataya Cha |
Namo Vishala Netraya Shushkodara Bhayakrite || 4 ||

Namah Pushkala Gatraya Sthula Romne'tha Vai Namah |
Namo Dirghaya Shushkaya Kaladamshtra Namo'stu Te || 5 ||

Namaste Kotarakshaya Durniriksyaya Vai Namah |
Namo Ghoraya Raudraya Bhishanaya Karaline || 6 ||

Namaste Sarva Bhakshaya Valimukha Namo'stu Te |
Suryaputra Namaste'stu Bhaskare Bhayadaya Cha || 7 ||

Adhodrishte Namaste'stu Samvartaka Namo'stu Te |
Namo Mandagate Tubhyam Nistrimshaya Namo'stu Te || 8 ||

Prasadam Kuru Me Saure Varado'si Mameshvara |
Etair Namabhir Uktaishcha Stutavan Yashcha Manavah |
Tasya Naiva Bhavet Pida Prito Bhava Dayanidhe || 9 ||

|| Iti Shri Shani Dasharatha Krita Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
कोणस्थ, पिंगल, बभ्रु, कृष्ण, रौद्र, अन्तक, यम, सौरि, शनैश्चर, मन्द — ये शनि के दस नाम हैं जिनकी पिप्पलाद मुनि ने स्तुति की। प्रातःकाल उठकर इन दस नामों का पाठ करने वाले को शनि की पीड़ा कभी नहीं होती।

दशरथ बोले: कृष्ण (श्यामवर्ण), नील, शितिकण्ठ (शिव) सदृश शनि को नमस्कार। कालाग्निरूप, कृतान्त (यमराज) को नमस्कार।

निर्मांस देह, दीर्घ श्मश्रु-जटाधारी, विशाल नेत्र, शुष्क उदर वाले भयंकर शनि को नमस्कार। पुष्कल शरीर, स्थूल रोम, दीर्घ, शुष्क, कालदंष्ट्र को नमस्कार। कोटराक्ष (गहरे नेत्र वाले), दुर्निरीक्ष्य, घोर, रौद्र, भीषण, कराल को नमस्कार।

सर्वभक्ष, वलीमुख, सूर्यपुत्र, भय देने वाले, अधोदृष्टि, संवर्तक, मन्दगति — हे सौरि! मुझ पर प्रसाद करो, तुम मेरे ईश्वर और वरदाता हो। इन नामों से जो स्तुति करता है उसे कभी पीड़ा नहीं होती।
benefits: Stotra composed by King Dasharatha to please Lord Shani
Contains 10 names of Shani that prevent all Saturn afflictions
Most powerful remedy for Sade Sati, Dhaiya and Shani Mahadasha
King Dasharatha received the boon that Shani would not trouble his devotees
Recite on Saturday morning for complete Shani shanti
description: Shani Dasharatha Stotram (शनि दशरथ कृत स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise dedicated to Lord Shani Dev. This sacred composition is sourced from Skanda Purana (Shani Mahatmya) and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Devotees recite this prayer to contain 10 names of Shani that prevent all Saturn afflictions and stotra composed by King Dasharatha to please Lord Shani. It is also said to recite on Saturday morning for complete Shani shanti. This prayer is traditionally recited on Saturday (Shanivar) and during Shani Jayanti and Shani Amavasya to invoke divine grace. The text contains 9 verses and takes around 4 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Shani Dasharatha Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shani Dasharatha Stotram in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. King Dasharatha's powerful prayer with 10 Shani names for Sade Sati relief.

seoFaqs.question: What is the story behind the Shani Dasharatha Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shani Dasharatha Stotram has a famous narrative recorded in classical tradition. When Shani was about to enter the Rohini nakshatra — a transit considered catastrophic for the kingdom of Ayodhya, capable of causing twelve years of drought and famine — King Dasharatha is said to have ascended his celestial chariot and confronted Shani in the heavens. Recognising the futility of battle against the slow-moving lord of karma, Dasharatha composed this hymn of praise on the spot. Pleased by the king's wisdom and devotion, Shani granted a boon that he would never afflict any devotee who recited this stotra with faith. The narrative is preserved in Padma Purana and Brahma Vaivarta tradition, and the hymn is consequently regarded as one of the most powerful Saturday recitations. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Hindupedia Shani; Drikpanchang.)

seoFaqs.question: Who composed the Shani Dasharatha Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition attributes the composition directly to King Dasharatha of Ayodhya, the father of Lord Rama, making it one of the few stotras whose author is said to be a Suryavanshi monarch rather than a rishi or acharya. The stotra opens with namaskara to Shani by twenty-nine alternative names — Konastha, Pingala, Babhru, Krishna, Roudra, Antaka, Yama, Saurih, Shanaishchara, Manda, Pippaladasammitah and others — drawn from puranic name-lists. The composition itself is preserved in classical tradition as part of the Shani Mahatmya appearing in Padma Purana and Brahma Vaivarta Purana sections, and is reproduced in the Skanda Purana navagraha chapters as well. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Vedadhara; Hindupedia.)

seoFaqs.question: When should one recite the Shani Dasharatha Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Saturday (Shanivar) is the universally accepted weekday for this stotra, ideally recited at sunrise or during the Shani-hora window. Shani Jayanti, falling on Vaishakha Amavasya, is the supreme annual occasion and many devotees recite the stotra 108 times that day. Shani Amavasya — when Amavasya itself falls on a Saturday — is similarly powerful. Other recommended occasions include the start of Sade Sati (the seven-and-a-half-year transit of Shani over the natal moon), the Dhaiya periods, and any time Shani transits a new rashi or goes retrograde. Devotees experiencing legal difficulties, chronic illness, employment loss, or planetary affliction in their kundali are advised to recite it daily. (Sources: Drikpanchang Shani Jayanti; Hindupedia Sade Sati; Stotra Nidhi.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Shani Dasharatha Stotram phalashruti promise?
seoFaqs.answer: The phalashruti of the Dasharatha Krita Shani Stotram promises that one who recites the hymn with devotion is freed from the effects of Sade Sati and Dhaiya, gains the favour of Shani Dev, escapes graha-pida (planetary affliction), and is protected from accidents, untimely death and chronic Saturn-related illnesses such as joint disease, paralysis and depression. The stotra specifically promises relief during the otherwise dreaded Rohini transit, since this was the very transit Dasharatha sought to avert. Material rewards include restoration of lost wealth, employment and reputation, while spiritual rewards include the gradual burning of accumulated karma and progress on the path to moksha. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Vedadhara; Hindupedia.)

seoFaqs.question: How is the Dasharatha Shani Stotra different from the Nilanjana Samabhasam shloka?
seoFaqs.answer: The Nilanjana Samabhasam is a single dhyana shloka from the Vyasa-krita Navagraha Stotra used at the start of any Shani worship to visualise Shani as collyrium-coloured, son of Surya, born of Chhaya, and elder brother of Yama. It is short, easy to memorise and serves as an invocation. The Dasharatha Krita Shani Stotram is a much longer hymn of praise, opening with the twenty-nine names of Shani and continuing through eight to ten verses of stuti and a phalashruti. Devotees commonly use the Nilanjana shloka as the dhyana at the beginning, then proceed to recite the full Dasharatha stotra as the main paath. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Hindupedia; Drikpanchang.)

seoFaqs.question: What offerings traditionally accompany the Shani Dasharatha Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The Saturday puja that frames this stotra traditionally uses sesame oil (til tel) for the lamp, often a four-wicked or chaturmukha lamp; black sesame seeds; urad dal; black or dark blue cloth; iron items; mustard oil; blue Aparajita or Shami flowers; and a naivedya of til-laddu, urad khichdi or kala chana. Devotees light the sesame-oil lamp before a Shani murti or yantra, ideally at a Shani temple or Shaneshwara shrine such as Shani Shingnapur, and circumambulate seven and a half times to symbolise Sade Sati. Equally important non-ritual components include feeding crows (Shani's vahana), serving the elderly and disabled, and supporting labourers on Saturdays. (Sources: Drikpanchang Shanivar; Hindupedia; Sanatan Sanstha.)

seoFaqs.question: Can the Shani Dasharatha Stotram be recited by women and children?
seoFaqs.answer: Yes. As a Pauranika stotra preserved in the Padma and Brahma Vaivarta Purana traditions, the Dasharatha Krita Shani Stotram is open to all devotees regardless of gender, caste or age — no diksha or initiation is required. Women across North India recite it during Shani Trayodashi vrats and on Saturdays for the welfare of husbands, sons and the household. Children are taught it before examinations and competitions where Shani-related obstacles like delays or unfair treatment are feared. The only customary requirements are cleanliness, an east or north-facing seat, and reciting with faith before a Shani image, yantra or simply with closed eyes facing the sun. (Sources: Hindupedia; Stotra Nidhi; Drikpanchang.)
